title: Quantum fluctuations and chaos in fully connected spin models
authors: Aleksandra A. Ziolkowska, Aleksandr N. Mikheev
abstract: We investigate beyond-mean-field dynamics in a fully connected $\mathrm{SU}(3)$ spin-exchange model, focusing on the interplay between chaotic dynamics and quantum fluctuations. Using the two-particle irreducible (2PI) effective action formalism, we derive equations of motion that systematically account for higher-order correlations generated by interactions, and demonstrate how quantum fluctuations can regularize chaotic dynamics displayed by macroscopic observables. Our results show that an accurate treatment of fluctuations is essential for describing macroscopic dynamics in quantum many-body systems and promote 2PI as a robust framework for connecting microscopic correlations to macroscopic nonequilibrium phenomena.
summary: This paper demonstrates that quantum fluctuations can regularize chaotic dynamics in a fully connected SU(3) spin model. By using the 2PI effective action formalism, the authors show that higher-order correlations are essential to capture the relaxation and smoothing of chaotic trajectories that standard mean-field or cumulant methods fail to describe.
main_problem: Investigating how quantum fluctuations regularize chaotic dynamics in the macroscopic observables of many-body systems and addressing the failure of standard cumulant expansions in strong-correlation regimes.
main_result: The 2PI effective action formalism successfully captures the regularization of chaos and relaxation processes that are missed by second-order cumulant expansions, which instead predict unphysical persistent oscillations.
method: The authors use the 2PI effective action formalism within the Schwinger-Keldysh framework, employing a 1/L expansion and Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation to derive self-consistent nonequilibrium Dyson equations.
model_or_system: A fully connected SU(3) spin-exchange model, which serves as an effective description for N-level systems coupled to a common bosonic mediator, such as in cavity-QED or trapped-ion systems.
key_observables: Macroscopic collective spins, two-time correlation functions, bosonic level populations, and the Frobenius norm of trajectory divergence (to diagnose chaos).
important_parameters: Interaction strengths (g+, g-), magnetic field (h), system size (L), and the inverse system size (1/L) acting as an effective Planck constant.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be homogeneous and the study focuses on U(1)-symmetric states; the 1/L correction due to operator-ordering subtleties is assumed to be negligible.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a dynamical phase diagram of regular vs. chaotic regimes; Figure 2 and 3 present diagrammatic notation for the 2PI formalism; Figure 4 shows the divergence of trajectories (Lyapunov-like growth); Figure 6 compares the relaxation of coherences in 2PI vs. cumulant expansion.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SU(3) model and the problem of chaos regularization, details the 2PI and cumulant methodologies, presents numerical results comparing relaxation and chaos suppression, and concludes with a discussion on the superiority of the 2PI framework.
why_interesting: It provides a robust theoretical framework (2PI) for understanding how quantum effects can suppress classical chaos, which is crucial for controlling many-body dynamics in platforms like cavity-QED and trapped ions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A sketch of the dynamical phase diagram for the SU(3) spin-exchange Hamiltonian initialized in a chaotic state for fixed h = g−= 1. All parameters refer to Eq. (1) in the main text. Phase (I) represents regular dynamics, phase (II) chaotic dynamics as indicated by the second-order cumulant expansion, and phase (III) designates chaotic dynamics as shown by the 2PI computations with next-to- leading order corrections. The upper limit of the graph with an in- finite system size corresponds to the mean-field limit, which is the same in both approaches. The dashed line corresponds to the ratio g+/g−= 0.2 analyzed in Fig. 4. See Sec. IV for the detailed discus- sion of the phase diagram.

FIG. 3. Diagrams contributing to the 2PI effective action considered in this work. (a) The only diagram contributing at leading order O(L) in the 1/L expansion. (b) Time-local diagram contributing at next- to-leading order O(L0). (c) An infinite series of next-to-leading-order diagrams. These include the time-local diagram (b) as well as an infinite number of time-nonlocal processes.

FIG. 4. Time dependence of the logarithm of the Frobenius- norm distance between trajectories for chaotic initial conditions and Hamiltonian parameters h = 1, g+ = 0.2, and g−= 1. We compare three system sizes within the NLO 2PI, the second-order cumulant expansion, and the mean-field approximations.

FIG. 5. Time dependence of the normalized bosonic level popula- tions for h = 1, g+ = 2, g−= 1 and the system size L = 15. The NLO 2PI approximation captures relaxation of the populations, whereas the second-order cumulant expansion exhibits persistent oscillations.

FIG. 6. Time dependence of the off-diagonal two-point bosonic correlations for chaotic initial conditions, Hamiltonian parameters h = 1, g+ = 2, g−= 1, and the system size L = 15. We plot the time-evolution of the real part of the coherences (left panel) and the imaginary part of the coherences (right panel) as computed within the 2PI and the cumulant expansion approach. The 2PI calculations indicate relaxation of one of the observables, whereas the cumulant expansion shows persistent oscillations for every quantity considered.
